coordinating_unit: School of Earth and Environmental Sciences
course_description: This course provides an advanced level of instruction and analysis on carbon and energy management principles and practices. Carbon and energy management is concerned with how society can best respond to climate change. A competent response to climate change requires a sound understanding of climate science and what the impacts of climate change are likely to be. A competent response to climate change also requires an understanding of how to manage different sources of carbon emissions, including the advantages and disadvantages of different power supply technologies and the management of carbon stocks in forests, soils and agriculture. We also need to know how to evaluate and compare the merits of different climate change response strategies and how Governments can promote effective responses through sound climate and energy policy. The course also includes instruction on a number of important carbon and energy management practices, such as carbon foot-printing, abatement and offset project design and appraisal, energy use auditing, alternative energy pathway design and appraisal, and emissions trading.
timetable:
Timetables are available on the UQ Public Timetable.

Access your timetable and preference your classes through My Timetable in my.UQ .

Next 2. Aims
course_aims: The aim of this course is to improve students' understanding of carbon and energy management to enable them to competently engage in responses to climate change.

learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate an understanding of key carbon and energy management competencies related to climate systems and drivers of climate change.
Demonstrate an understanding of key carbon and energy management competencies related to carbon inventory and the management of emissions reduction opportunities.
Demonstrate an understanding of key carbon and energy management competencies related to sustainable power supply systems.
Demonstrate an understanding of key carbon and energy management competencies related to carbon in natural resources.

Demonstrate an understanding of key carbon and energy management competencies related to carbon offsetting principles and practise, climate and energy policy and the role of insurance and finance in climate change responses.

assessment_summary.title: Week 9 Case Study on Carbon Management

assessment_details.title: Week 9 Case Study on Carbon Management
assessment_details.task_description:
Case Study Assessment ENVM7524 Semester 2 2021

Contents

1. Preface

2. Task Instructions, Word Limit and Report Structure

3. Due Dates

4. Submission Method and Marking System

5. Formatting and Referencing Requirements

1. Preface

Note: The assessment for ENVM7524 is different to ENVM1522. The assessment for ENVM7524 is set for Masters-level expectations and learning outcomes. Be sure to read the assignment instructions and criteria carefully. They differ from the assignment instructions and criteria used for ENVM1522 in numerous ways.

Background: Many firms are responding to society’s call for action on climate change by going ‘carbon neutral’. Indeed, terms like ‘carbon neutral’, ‘net zero’ and ‘carbon positive’ are often used interchangeably to describe how a firm estimates its reportable greenhouse gas emissions and then takes steps to reduce those emissions . Firms can emit and reduce emissions at different scales, have different emissions reduction priorities, use different emissions reduction methods and be at different stages in their pathway towards having zero net reportable emissions. In this assignment you will examine how a specified firm has estimated and reduced its reportable emissions. The objective is for you to develop and demonstrate your understanding of the actions and issues involved in the process of carbon management

2. Task Instructions, Word Limit and Report Structure

Each student will be assigned (via blackboard) a case study firm by the end of Week 1.

ENVM7524 students will be allocated case study firms that require a more advanced level of carbon management analysis to the firms allocated to ENVM1522 students for their equivalent assessment activity.

Each student is required to complete the assignment on their assigned case study firm.

No two students should complete the assignment on the same case study firm.

Students who enrol after Week 1 or for whatever reason have not been assigned a case study firm by the end of Week 1, must email the course coordinator as soon as possible so they can be assigned a case study firm.

Your task is to examine how your case study firm has estimated and reduced its reportable greenhouse gas emissions. To do this, you must follow the Report Structure outlined below and include the content listed in the Report Structure below for each relevant section.

Case studies are a research method commonly used in environmental management. The format of case study analysis varies based on the research purpose and context. In this assignment, follow the Report Structure provided below. Case study analysis allows detailed examination and explanation of an interesting example of an important and often complex phenomenon. In this assignment, the interesting example is your case study firm, and the phenomenon is how the firm engages in the process of estimating and reducing its net reportable greenhouse gas emissions

The recommended word length for the assignment is 3000 words . This excludes the title, abstract, all figures and tables and their titles, footnotes, citations, and the reference list. There is no penalty for exceeding the recommended word limit.

Your report must follow the Report Structure outlined below.

Section

Content

Word Length

Title

You can choose your own title. Good titles have meaning and give the reader insight into what the report is about. For example, ‘Assignment 1 ENVM7524’ has no meaning, but this one does; ‘How do banks manage carbon emissions not only directly from their office operations, but also from their investments? A case study of Westpac’.

No more than 30 words.

Abstract

An Abstract is a summary of your report. Ideally, it should have five parts: (1) why carbon management is an important topic for investigation; (2) why your case study firm is an interesting example of carbon management; (3) what are the firm’s emissions; (4) how has the firm reduced those emissions; and (5) what are the lessons or implications or next steps?

No more than 250 words. Anything longer loses its usefulness as a summary.

Introduction

This section should address three things: (1) why is carbon management important (here is a chance to summarise relevant academic literature); (2) why is your case study firm an interesting example of carbon management; and if applicable (3) what special or interesting aspect of carbon management does our report focus?

No more than 500 words.

Overview of the Firm

Try and be very concise and descriptive. What does your firm do? What sector does it work in? Where does it work? How ‘big’ is it (e.g. turnover, employees)? Are there any climate change issues that are particularly relevant to the firm?

No more than 250 words.

Pledges

What are the firm’s emissions reduction pledges or targets? Is it possible to explain the key reasons behind why the firm has made these pledges?

No more than 250 words.

Emissions Estimates

Use tables and figures to display data. Use concise, descriptive text to explain the data presented in the illustrations. Are they organisational or product footprint estimates? If applicable and possible: categorise emissions by scope or source; show changes over time; and compare to sector wide performance and contextualise the estimates.

No more than 250 words.

Emissions Reductions

This is an important section in the report and one where ENVM7524 students are expected to demonstrate a deeper, more advanced understanding of carbon management practice compared to students in ENVM1522. The recommended word count for this section is longer for ENVM7524 students. The corresponding markeing criteria for ENVM7524 are different to ENVM1522 and reflect Masters-level learning expectations. In this section you should summarise how the firm reduced their reportable emissions. Did the firm use one method of emissions reduction or more? If more than one, how do the methods compare in terms of amount of reductions achieved and cost? Can you present any data on the cost of emissions reductions? If possible, pick one or two of the methods used and explain what was involved. For example, if they bought offsets, what sorts of offsets did they buy or if they implemented an abatement project, how were emissions reduced?

No more than 750 words.

Performance Appraisal

This is another important section in the report and one where ENVM7524 students are expected to demonstrate a deeper, more advanced understanding of carbon management practice compared to students in ENVM1522. The recommended word count for this section is longer for ENVM7524 students. The corresponding markeing criteria for ENVM7524 are different to ENVM1522 and reflect Masters-level learning expectations. In this section you should discuss whether (in your assessment) the firm did a good job with their carbon management. You should express your opinion and support your argument with explanation and evidence. Consider how you might assess good carbon management performance? Can you compare the firm’s performance across the sector? How rigorous and reliable are the emissions estimates? How effective have the carbon management efforts been in terms of achieving emissions reduction targets, addressing climate risks, and supporting the firm’s license to operate and ability to grow new business?

No more than 750 words.

Conclusion

Your conclusion should include: a summary of your analysis; a short statement of the limitations of your analysis; and, if relevant, a short list of recommendations for what the firm should do to improve its carbon management performance.

No more than 250 words.

References

You need to support your analysis and appraisal with evidence and that evidence needs to be referenced. It is usually best to use more than the firm’s reports as the source of your information. Diversity of references is important. It is a good idea to use peer reviewed journals as references but in this assignment, those are probably best suited to the Introduction and Appraisal sections.

There is no minimum number of references required for this assignment.

3. Due Dates

The assignment must be submitted before 12pm (midday) Brisbane time on Monday 20th September 2021.

Extensions must be negotiated in advance of the due date. Approval will only be granted in exceptional circumstances and the new deadline will be absolute.

Failure to submit by the due date will incur a penalty of 10% of the assessment item per day late.

Note: to be fair and consistent, submissions made any time after 12pm 20th September (including only a few minutes after) but before 12pm 21st September will incur a one day 10% late penalty.

4. Submission Method Marking System

This assignment is worth 50% of your final course grade .

The assignment will be marked out of 50 against the relevant Marking Criteria listed Section 5 of the course profile.

Make your submission as a word document or pdf.

Make your submission via the Turnitin link on the course blackboard page before the due date.

Include your name and student number under the title on the first page.

5. Formatting and Referencing Requirements

Please do not use a cover page. The first page of your submission should have the title at the top, centred, bold and in Times New Roman 12 size font. Your name and student number should be under the title name. The Abstract should follow directly under, followed by the Introduction etc.

No need to start Sections on new pages.

Do not include attachments. Any material included as attachments will not be assessed.

Consider using a numbering system for sections and sub-sections but do not number the Title or Abstract.

For the body of the report use Times New Roman, 12 size font, single spacing, justified format, normal margins. Put section headings in bold.

Do not cut and paste material directly from the lecture notes or other sources. Use your own words. Be sure to cite and list references correctly. Learn how to reference if you do not know (as the UQ Library).

Do not cut and paste illustrations from other sources . Represent the illustration using your own interpretation - redraw it and modify it if needed to best illustrate your point, but be sure to cite the original source of the illustration.

No not use dot points in this assignment.

UQ plagiarism rules apply to this assignment.

You are required to complete this work individually. Co-authored submissions are not permitted.

Use Harvard referencing style.

assessment_details.title: Week 13 Online Quiz
assessment_details.task_description:
Online Quiz Assessment ENVM7524 Semester 2 2021

Contents

1. Quiz Instructions, Quiz Format and Word Limits

2. Due Dates (Quiz Period) and Late Penalties

3. Submission Method and Marking System

4. Formatting and Referencing Requirements

Note: The Quiz used for ENVM7524 will include different questions to the one used for ENVM1522. The assessment for ENVM7524 is set for Masters-level expectations and learning outcomes. Be sure to read the Quiz instructions, questions and marking criteria carefully. Whilst the arrangements, structure and format of the ENVM7524 Quiz is similar to ENVM1522, the questions will be different.

1. Quiz Instructions, Quiz Format and Word Limits

Each student is required to complete an online quiz in Week 13 (dates listed below).

This is an individual assessment. Students must submit their own work.

Students will have 48hrs to complete and submit their answers to the quiz questions (hereon called the ‘quiz period’) from the time the quiz is made available to all students via the course blackboard site (dates and times listed below).

Students are not permitted to collude with others on quiz answers during the quiz period.

The quiz will cover all material covered in the course between Weeks 1 and 12.

The quiz will include ten questions worth five marks each.

The quiz will be marked out of 50.

The quiz is open book; students may refer to lecture notes and other references for information.

Students are to write and compile their answers in their own answer document (word or pdf).

Answers can be in the form of dot points, tables, figures, other illustrations (e.g concept diagrams), or detailed solutions (e.g., a carbon footprint estimate with working shown).

The revision questions listed at the end of each week’s lecture notes will be useful revision.

There is no word limit for any of the answers to the quiz per se, but recommended word lengths for answers may be provided on some questions.

You must get a mark of 50% or higher in the quiz (25/50 or greater) to pass the course (see Assessment Hurdle listed in the course profile).

2. Due Dates and Late Penalties

The quiz will be provided to students via the course blackboard site at 12pm Brisbane time on Wednesday 27th October 2021.

Students will need to submit their answers by 12pm Brisbane time on Friday 29th October 2021.

The time between quiz release (12pm Brisbane time on Wednesday 27th October 2021) and the due date for submission (12pm Brisbane time on Friday 29th October 2021) will be referred to as the ‘quiz period’.

Students will be sent an email to their UQ email account when the quiz is made available.

For those unable to attend the exam due to illness or circumstances beyond their control please contact the Course Coordinator as soon as possible to make alternative arrangements.

Extensions must be negotiated in advance of the due date.

Failure to submit by 12pm Brisbane time on Friday 29th October 2021 will incur a penalty of 10% of the assessment item per day late.

Note: to be fair and consistent, submissions made any time after 12pm Brisbane time on Friday 29th October 2021 (including only a few minutes after) but before 12pm 30th October will incur a one day 10% late penalty.

3. Submission Method and Marking System

This assignment is worth 50% of your final course grade.

Each question will have five elements to a fully correct answer, each element corresponding to one mark (each question will be worth five marks).

Make your submission as a word document or pdf.

Make your submission via the Turnitin link on the course blackboard page before the due date.

Include this title as a title at the top of the first page; ‘ENVM7524 Sem 2 2021 Assignment 2 Online Quiz Answers’.

Include your name and student number directly under the title at the top of the first page.

4. Formatting and Referencing Requirements

Please do not use a cover page. The first page of your submission should have the title at the top, centred, bold and in Times New Roman 12 size font. Your name and student number should be under the title name. Your answers should start directly under.

List your answers in order from Q1 to Q10.

No need to start answers to different questions on new pages.

Do not include attachments. Any material included as attachments will not be assessed.

For the text of your answers use Times New Roman, 12 size font, single spacing, justified format, normal margins. Put question headings in bold.

Do not cut and paste material directly from the lecture notes or other sources. Use your own words.

Referencing is not required but citing information sources and references where relevant is likely to contribute to a more complete and correct answer.

Do not cut and paste illustrations from other sources . Represent the illustration using your own interpretation - redraw it and modify it if needed to best illustrate your point. No need to cite the original source of the illustration for the purposes of this assignment.

UQ plagiarism rules apply to this assignment.

You are required to complete this work individually. Co-authored submissions are not permitted.

If you choose to include references, use Harvard referencing style.

learning_resources.university_learning_resources:
Access to required and recommended resources, plus past central exam papers, is available at the UQ Library website ( http://www.library.uq.edu.au/lr/ENVM7524 ).

The University offers a range of resources and services to support student learning. Details are available on the myUQ website ( https://my.uq.edu.au/ ).
learning_resources.school_learning_resources:
Computer laboratories

The School offers an extensive range of computing resources in several laboratories across the Chamberlain Building (35), Steele Building (3) and Richards Building (5) including:

the Geographic Information Systems (GIS) and Remote Sensing (RS) Lab (Room 35-401)

the Geological Science computing labs (Rooms 03-221/222)

the General Computing Lab (Room 35-432)

the Planning Studio (Rooms 03-314/315)

the dedicated Postgrad only Computing Space (Room 35-402)

These labs provide access to: general productivity (MS Office, MS Project, Endnote etc.) and specialised applications, including general and scientific statistics (SPSS, Matlab, nVivo, R) and demographic and climatological analysis software; 3D and graphic design; Remote Sensing (ERDAS Imagine; ENVI/IDL; Trimble Definiens); Life Cycle Analysis; GIS (all ESRI ArcGIS products); and PAST, Geochemist Workbench and MapInfo. Other applications may be available as requested for practicals. Data used in courses is stored in the shared disk space. Several gigabytes of spatial GIS data, including Census data, and airborne/satellite images are accessible from either the file server or data CDs.

Students also can access Faculty of Science computer labs and Interactive Learning spaces in Building 69 (see http://www.uq.edu.au/ilc/ for more information).

IT/AV Equipment and Teaching Facilities

A range of equipment is also available for student use including: A4 and A3 document printing and scanning; 1.1m wide sheet scanner; and access to a large format inkjet printer (posters up to 1.5m wide).

The SEES Planning Studio in the Steele Building (3-314/315) is a high-tech, collaborative, flat-floored teaching space which offers students the most advanced studio and collaborative learning experiences available. The school also has a fleet of large format tablets, iPads and Microsoft Surface’s for use in teaching classes.

Scientific Wet Laboratories and Microscopes

The wet chemistry and microscopy laboratories in the Chamberlain (35-523) and Steele Buildings (03-221/222/228/229 & 321) contain equipment to assist marine chemists, hydrologists, geochemists and petrologists and palynologists perform wet chemical and instrumental analytical techniques. A comprehensive collection of modern optical microscopes is available plus two Scanning Electron Microscopes and a portable X-ray fluorescence gun for geochemical analysis. Students can measure stable isotope ratios and study particle size, distribution and shape – techniques useful in climate and hydrological studies. Further information on the School’s computing, physical laboratory and field sampling equipment is available at sees.uq.edu.au.

Social media

Please be aware that photographs taken in this course may be used for SEES social media and promotional purposes. Please inform your course coordinator if you do not wish to be included.

Field trip participation

The School offers a significant number of courses that conduct field trips. In courses that have single day field trips which provide charter bus travel, students will be typically charged a $10 - $30 non-refundable fee if they travel to the field site on the charter bus. In courses that have overnight field trips and require accommodation, meals etc., additional fees will be payable. Fee waivers will only be considered on the basis of financial hardship and will require supporting documentation. These courses will specify the amount in their ECP and it will be communicated to students by the lecturer.

Travel relating to course placement, field trip or work experience

The impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic have changed the way that a number of our activities are managed, including clinical and industry based placements and field trips. To mitigate travel related risks, UQ requires all students to complete an online Travel Declaration prior to undertaking approved placements, work experience or field trips where the activity requires:

1. travel more than 50km from your usual place of residence; and

2. an overnight stay (minimum of one night)

The Declaration outlines your responsibilities while engaging in this travel and also provides information on the UQ processes in place to ensure your safety and support while engaging in your placement or field trip. By completing the Declaration you acknowledge that you have read, understood, and accepted these responsibilities. Failure to complete the declaration will exclude you from participating in the travel and associated activities. Further details can be found on both the UQ website and UQ Travel Insurance for Students brochure .

If you have any questions regarding your specific placement or field trip circumstances please contact the Course Coordinator.

Learning resources

The School has developed a range of useful student learning resources including:

Handbooks on essay writing, report writing and presenting graphical information

Guides on Harvard and Chicago referencing styles

Online tutorials on: referencing and graphical presentation

All assessment must comply with these minimum standards. The documents are available on the Student Support section of the School website

Library contacts

The School of Earth and Environmental Sciences has two Library Client Services librarians who look after the areas of Planning (Anna Bagshaw: a.bagshaw@library.uq.edu.au ), and Geology, Geophysics, Geochemistry, Geography, Environmental Science and Environmental Management, Paleontology, and Occupational Health & Safety Science (Kellie Ashley: k.ashley@library.uq.edu.au ).

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the University's assessment-related procedures can be found on my.UQ. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/services/exams-and-assessment )

Academic Integrity

It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Academic Integrity Modules - Support for Students

It may seem simple – don’t cheat and do reference your work. However, there are many issues involved in understanding what is meant by academic integrity and how you could be misled into engaging in an action which may be academic misconduct. The Academic Integrity Modules (AIM) will enable you to uphold academic integrity throughout your studies at UQ and beyond. From Semester 1, 2021, completion of the modules in your first semester of enrolment will be compulsory for all commencing students.

Plagiarism

The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Student Integrity and Misconduct policy ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.60.04-student-integrity-and-misconduct ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

An application for an Extension of Assessment Due Date for medical grounds and/or other exceptional circumstances shall be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation by the date specified in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile. Students will be notified of the outcome of their request via the myRequests section of my.UQ (and an email sent to their student account).

Additional details associated with extension requests, including acceptable and unacceptable reasons, may be found at my.UQ .

Request for Deferral of Exams

A request for deferral of an exam must be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation via mySI-net > my Requests no later than five (5) calendar days after the date of the original exam. Students are encouraged to familiarise themselves with the information on deferring an exam available on my.UQ .

Feedback on Assessment

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the my.UQ web site. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/academic-progress-and-final-results/querying-result )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

Supplementary assessment

A supplementary assessment is designed to give you a second chance at achieving a passing grade for a course, should you receive a final course grade of 3 (or N subject to the Supplementary Assessment Procedures ). It isn't a repeat of an exam, or a specific assignment – it's designed to test the learning outcomes for the entire course, or specific learning outcomes tailored to you.

Supplementary assessment can take any form (e.g. oral, written, examination etc.), and the highest grade you can receive is a 4 (or P). Supplementary assessment isn't available for every course and is only granted in accordance with the Supplementary Assessment Procedures .

Please check the course profile to see if your course is fully or partially exempt from supplementary assessment. If you have any questions about supplementary assessment, or if you're unsure whether you qualify, please contact staff in your school office or faculty office.

Course grade cut-offs

Any change to grade cut-offs will be in accordance with PPL 3.10.02 Assessment – Procedures .

School of Earth and Environmental Sciences
Assessment Guidelines

PLAGIARISM and ACADEMIC MISCONDUCT WILL NOT BE TOLERATED :

You should be aware that the University employs purpose built software to detect plagiarism. It is very important that you understand clearly the practical meaning of plagiarism. All students are required to complete the Academic Integrity Tutorial, available at the following link: https://www.uq.edu.au/integrity/ . Students are required to understand their roles and responsibilities as students, as stated in the UQ Student Charter .

DEFINITION OF PLAGIARISM:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

EXAMPLES OF PLAGIARISM:

1. Direct copying of paragraphs, sentences, a single sentence or significant parts of a sentence;

2. Direct copying of paragraphs, sentences, a single sentence or significant parts of a sentence with an end reference but without quotation marks around the copied text;

3. Copying ideas, concepts, research results, computer codes, statistical tables, designs, images, sounds or text or any combination of these;

4. Paraphrasing, summarising or simply rearranging another person's words, ideas, etc. without changing the basic structure and/or meaning of the text;

5. Offering an idea or interpretation that is not one's own without identifying whose idea or interpretation it is;

6. A 'cut and paste' of statements from multiple sources;

7. Presenting as independent, work done in collaboration with others;

8. Copying or adapting another student's original work into a submitted assessment item.

SUPPLEMENTARY ASSESSMENT:

Supplementary assessment will be designed to allow eligible students to have another chance to demonstrate they have achieved specific learning objectives of the course. In order to receive a grade of 3S4 (Pass) students will be required to achieve a mark of 50% or greater in this assessment. Further information can be found through the my.UQ website .

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses

Students on a placement course – also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice – should refer to the University’s Work Integrated Learning and Work Experience Policy ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.04-work-integrated-learning-and-work-experience ) for detailed information.

Working with Children

Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/1.60.07-working-children ) to find out how to apply for a ‘blue card’.

Students with a Disability

Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Advisor at Student Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.08-alternative-academic-arrangements-students-disability ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.09-arrangements-reasonable-adjustments-examinations-students-disability#Procedures ).

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety

Undergraduate and Postgraduate Students should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/2.30.14-occupational-health-and-safety-laboratory ).

Fitness to Practise

Students enrolled in programs and courses that include a practical placement component (also known as work integrated learning, clinical immersion, clinical placement, clinical practice, externship, fieldwork, industry experience, industry study, internship, practicum, teaching practice, work placement) should refer to the University policy Fitness to Practise ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.30.14-fitness-practise ) for detailed information on issues of competence and on management of concerns relating to patient and public safety.

Previous 5. Assessment

Next Summary
course_introduction:
The course is structured around twelve important understandings of carbon and energy management. Those are; (1) understanding how climate systems work; (2) understanding the drivers and evidence of human-induced climate change; (3) understanding the expected impacts of climate change and principles of climate risk assessment and climate justice; (4) understanding how the public and private sectors respond to climate change; (5) understanding carbon inventory including emissions by country and source, currently and historically, plus footprinting methods for countries, organisations, products and services; (6) understanding the links between carbon, energy and sustainable electricity supply systems including key principles and terms, and issues of scale in sustainable electricity supply system design; (7) understanding the advantages and disadvantages of power supply technologies including coal, oil and gas (conventional and unconventional), nuclear, hydroelectric, solar, wind, wave, tidal, geothermal and bioenergy and topics of special interest such as carbon capture and storage (including negative emissions technologies, direct air carbon capture and storage and bioenergy carbon capture and storare), hydrogen fuel, batteries and electric vehicles; (8) understanding carbon stored in natural resources including methods to estimate carbon stocks and sequestration rates in soils and vegetation (particular planted and natural forests) and the opportunities and limitations for the use of nature-based responses to climate change including climate-smart agriculture; (9) understanding carbon offsetting principles, processes and practise including major offsetting schemes, concepts of co-benefits, blue carbon and REDD+; (10) understanding methods to assess emissions reduction options including marginal abatement cost curves, basic project financial appraisal methods, total systems cost analysis for electricity supply systems, the social cost of carbon and the works of William Nordhaus; (11) understanding climate and energy policy, including key international frameworks such as the UNFCCC and related initiatives, insights from the history of climate and energy policy in Australia, and lessons from noteworthy initiatives such as the Australian Renewable Energy Target and the Green Climate Fund; and (12) understanding the role of insurance and finance in responses to climate change, including the influence of the Taskforce for Climate-related Financial Disclosures, and insights from government-led climate finance initiatives such as the Australian Clean Energy Finance Corporation.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

In response to feedback from students we have modified the MACC exercise, modified the final piece of assessment, and included more material on practical aspects of energy management.

graduate_attributes.intro: Successfully completing this course will contribute to the recognition of your attainment of the following UQ (Postgrad Coursework) graduate attributes:

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A2 . A broad understanding of the field of study, including how other disciplines relate to the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A3 . A comprehensive and in-depth knowledge in the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A5 . An international perspective on the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A7 . An appreciation of the link between theory and practice .
B1 . The ability to collect , analyse and organise information and ideas and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently , in both written and spoken forms .
B2 . The ability to interact effectively with others in order to work towards a common outcome.
B3 . The ability to select and use the appropriate level , style and means of communication .
B4 . The ability to engage effectively and appropriately with information and communication technologies .
B5 . The ability to practise as part of an interdisciplinary team .
C2 . The ability to work and learn independently and effectively .
C3 . The ability to generate ideas and adapt innovatively to changing environments.
C5 . The ability to formulate and investigate problems , create solutions , innovate and improve current practices .
C6 . The abilities and skills that provide a foundation for future leadership roles.
D2 . The ability to apply critical reasoning to issues through independent thought and informed judgement .
D4 . The ability to process material and to critically analyse and integrate information from a wide range of sources.
D5 . The ability to evaluate opinions , make decisions and to reflect critically on the justifications for decisions using an evidence-based approach .
E1 . An understanding of social and civic responsibility .
E3 . An appreciation of the philosophical and social contexts of a discipline.
E4 . A knowledge and respect of ethics and ethical standards in relation to a major area of study.
E5 . A knowledge of other cultures and times and an appreciation of cultural diversity .
E7 . The ability to work effectively and sensitively across all areas of society .
E8 . An understanding of and respect for the roles and expertise of associated disciplines .

course_grading:
Example criteria for each of the grades can be found in PPL 3.10.02 Assessment Procedures - section 7 Appendix.

Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Low Fail: Absence of evidence of achievement of course learning outcomes:

1 - 24%

Grade 2, Fail: Minimal evidence of achievement of course learning outcomes:

25 - 44%

Grade 3, Marginal Fail: Demonstrated evidence of developing achievement of course learning outcomes:

45-49%

PERFORMANCE HURDLE: see Other Requirements and Comments below.

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrated evidence of functional achievement of course learning outcomes:

50-64%

PERFORMANCE HURDLE: see Other Requirements and Comments below.

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrated evidence of proficient achievement of course learning outcomes:

65-74%

PERFORMANCE HURDLE: see Other Requirements and Comments below.

Grade 6, Distinction: Demonstrated evidence of advanced achievement of course learning outcomes:

75-84%

PERFORMANCE HURDLE: see Other Requirements and Comments below.

Grade 7, High Distinction: Demonstrated evidence of exceptional achievement of course learning outcomes:

85% and above

PERFORMANCE HURDLE: see Other Requirements and Comments below.

Other Requirements & Comments:

PERFORMANCE HURDLE : Students are required to achieve a mark of 50% or greater in the Online Quiz to pass the course.
late_submission:
You are required to submit assessable items on time. Late submissions will incur a penalty of 10% per 24-hour period after the submission deadline.

Applications for Extensions

Extension applications must be received by the assessment due date and time. Your submission must be made via the link at below, not to your Course Coordinator. Supporting evidence must be provided.

Information on applying for an extension can be found here - my.UQ Applying for an extension

During busy periods there could be a delay in processing your extension. Please ensure that you continue to complete and submit your assessment to minimise late penalties if your extension application is denied.

If you have been ill or unable to attend class for more than 14 days, we advise you to carefully consider whether you are capable of successfully completing your courses this semester. You might be eligible to withdraw without academic penalty . We strongly recommend you seek advice from the faculty that administers your program.
other_assessment_information:
Supplementary Assessment

Should you fail a course with a grade of 3, or a non-graded ‘N’, you may be eligible for supplementary assessment. Refer to my.UQ for information on supplementary assessment.

Supplementary assessment may not be available for all courses, or for some of the assessment items for a course. The highest grade you can receive following supplementary assessment is a 4 or P. Details of availability of supplementary assessment for this course are set out below.

Supplementary Assessment is available for this Course .

Refer to my.UQ for how to apply for supplementary assessment.

Supplementary assessment can take any form, for example, an oral or a written exam. Students who are eligible and approved for a supplementary assessment and the form of assessment is an examination, are expected to be available to sit the supplementary exam during the University’s Deferred and Supplementary examination period. Once approved, supplementary assessment cannot be rescinded by the student.

Other

Performance hurdle: Students are required to achieve a mark of 50% or greater in the Online Quiz to pass the course.
